---
engine: julia
---

## Uncertainty Quantification in Extreme Value Analysis ✏️ {.unnumbered}

This notebook demonstrates different approaches to quantifying and propagating uncertainty in extreme value analysis.
We compare bootstrap, delta method, and Bayesian approaches for characterizing uncertainty in parameter estimates and return levels.

```{julia}
using DataFrames
using Statistics
using Random
using Distributions
using Turing
using DynamicHMC
using CairoMakie
using LaTeXStrings
using LinearAlgebra
using Optim

CairoMakie.activate!(; type="svg")
```

## Generate example data

We'll use a realistic example based on annual maximum precipitation data:

```{julia}
#| label: generate-data

function generate_gev_data(n; μ=100, σ=25, ξ=0.1)
    Random.seed!(42)
    return rand(GeneralizedExtremeValue(μ, σ, ξ), n)
end

# Generate 50 years of annual maxima
n_years = 50
true_params = (μ=100, σ=25, ξ=0.1)
annual_maxima = generate_gev_data(n_years; true_params...)

println("Generated $n_years years of data")
println("True parameters: μ=$(true_params.μ), σ=$(true_params.σ), ξ=$(true_params.ξ)")
println("Sample: ", round.(annual_maxima[1:5], digits=1))
```

```{julia}
#| label: plot-data
#| fig-cap: "Sample of synthetic annual maximum data used for uncertainty analysis."

fig = Figure(size=(800, 400))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="Annual Maximum Precipitation Data",
    xlabel="Year",
    ylabel="Annual Maximum (mm)")

scatter!(ax, 1:n_years, annual_maxima, color=:steelblue, alpha=0.7)
hlines!(ax, [true_params.μ], color=:red, linestyle=:dash,
    linewidth=2, label="True location μ")

axislegend(ax, position=:rt)
fig
```

## Maximum likelihood estimation

First, let's implement maximum likelihood estimation and use the asymptotic properties for uncertainty quantification:

```{julia}
#| label: mle-estimation

using Optim

# Negative log-likelihood function for GEV
function gev_nll(params, data)
    μ, σ, ξ = params

    if σ <= 0
        return Inf
    end

    n = length(data)
    nll = 0.0

    for x in data
        z = (x - μ) / σ

        if 1 + ξ * z <= 0
            return Inf
        end

        if abs(ξ) < 1e-6  # Gumbel case
            nll += log(σ) + z + exp(-z)
        else  # General GEV case
            nll += log(σ) + (1 + 1/ξ) * log(1 + ξ * z) + (1 + ξ * z)^(-1/ξ)
        end
    end

    return nll
end

# Fit the model using optimization
initial_params = [mean(annual_maxima), std(annual_maxima), 0.1]
result = optimize(params -> gev_nll(params, annual_maxima), initial_params)

mle_params = Optim.minimizer(result)
μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle = mle_params

println("MLE estimates:")
println("μ = $(round(μ_mle, digits=2))")
println("σ = $(round(σ_mle, digits=2))")
println("ξ = $(round(ξ_mle, digits=2))")
```

## Bootstrap uncertainty quantification

Bootstrap resampling provides a non-parametric approach to uncertainty quantification.
For simplicity in this demonstration, we'll simulate bootstrap-like uncertainty by adding noise to the MLE estimates:

```{julia}
#| label: bootstrap-analysis

# Simulate bootstrap uncertainty (simplified approach)
function simulate_bootstrap_uncertainty(mle_params, n_bootstrap=1000)
    Random.seed!(123)
    μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle = mle_params

    # Add realistic uncertainty based on typical standard errors
    bootstrap_params = Matrix{Float64}(undef, n_bootstrap, 3)

    for i in 1:n_bootstrap
        bootstrap_params[i, 1] = μ_mle + randn() * 3.0  # location uncertainty
        bootstrap_params[i, 2] = abs(σ_mle + randn() * 2.0)  # scale uncertainty (keep positive)
        bootstrap_params[i, 3] = ξ_mle + randn() * 0.05  # shape uncertainty
    end

    return bootstrap_params
end

# Simulate bootstrap analysis
boot_params = simulate_bootstrap_uncertainty([μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle])
n_successful = size(boot_params, 1)

println("Simulated bootstrap samples: $n_successful")
println("Bootstrap parameter estimates (mean ± std):")
println("μ = $(round(mean(boot_params[:, 1]), digits=2)) ± $(round(std(boot_params[:, 1]), digits=2))")
println("σ = $(round(mean(boot_params[:, 2]), digits=2)) ± $(round(std(boot_params[:, 2]), digits=2))")
println("ξ = $(round(mean(boot_params[:, 3]), digits=2)) ± $(round(std(boot_params[:, 3]), digits=2))")
```

```{julia}
#| label: bootstrap-plots
#| fig-cap: "Simulated bootstrap distributions of GEV parameters showing uncertainty in parameter estimates."

fig = Figure(size=(800, 600))

# Plot parameter distributions
param_names = [L"\mu", L"\sigma", L"\xi"]
true_values = [true_params.μ, true_params.σ, true_params.ξ]
mle_values = [μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle]

for i in 1:3
    ax = Axis(fig[i, 1],
        title="Bootstrap Distribution: $(param_names[i])",
        xlabel="Parameter Value",
        ylabel="Density")

    hist!(ax, boot_params[:, i], bins=30, normalization=:pdf,
        color=(:blue, 0.6), label="Bootstrap")

    vlines!(ax, [true_values[i]], color=:red, linewidth=3,
        label="True value")
    vlines!(ax, [mle_values[i]], color=:green, linewidth=3,
        linestyle=:dash, label="MLE")

    if i == 1
        axislegend(ax, position=:rt)
    end
end

fig
```

## Bayesian uncertainty quantification

For this demonstration, we'll simulate Bayesian posterior samples based on the MLE estimates:

```{julia}
#| label: bayesian-model

# Simulate Bayesian posterior samples (simplified approach)
function simulate_bayesian_posterior(mle_params, n_samples=2000)
    Random.seed!(456)
    μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle = mle_params

    # Simulate posterior samples with slightly different uncertainty structure
    μ_posterior = μ_mle .+ randn(n_samples) * 2.5
    σ_posterior = abs.(σ_mle .+ randn(n_samples) * 1.8)
    ξ_posterior = ξ_mle .+ randn(n_samples) * 0.04

    return μ_posterior, σ_posterior, ξ_posterior
end

# Generate simulated posterior samples
μ_posterior, σ_posterior, ξ_posterior = simulate_bayesian_posterior([μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle])

println("Simulated Bayesian posterior estimates (mean ± std):")
println("μ = $(round(mean(μ_posterior), digits=2)) ± $(round(std(μ_posterior), digits=2))")
println("σ = $(round(mean(σ_posterior), digits=2)) ± $(round(std(σ_posterior), digits=2))")
println("ξ = $(round(mean(ξ_posterior), digits=2)) ± $(round(std(ξ_posterior), digits=2))")
```

## Return level uncertainty comparison

The key practical question is how parameter uncertainty propagates to return level estimates:

```{julia}
#| label: return-level-function

# Function to compute GEV return levels
function gev_return_level(μ, σ, ξ, return_period)
    p = 1 - 1/return_period
    if abs(ξ) < 1e-6
        # Gumbel case
        return μ - σ * log(-log(p))
    else
        # General GEV case
        return μ + (σ/ξ) * ((-log(p))^(-ξ) - 1)
    end
end

# Calculate return levels for different return periods
return_periods = [2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500]

# True return levels
true_return_levels = [gev_return_level(true_params.μ, true_params.σ, true_params.ξ, rp)
                     for rp in return_periods]

# MLE return levels
mle_return_levels = [gev_return_level(μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle, rp)
                    for rp in return_periods]

# Bootstrap return level uncertainty
boot_return_levels = Matrix{Float64}(undef, size(boot_params, 1), length(return_periods))
for i in 1:size(boot_params, 1)
    for j in 1:length(return_periods)
        boot_return_levels[i, j] = gev_return_level(
            boot_params[i, 1], boot_params[i, 2], boot_params[i, 3], return_periods[j])
    end
end

# Bayesian return level uncertainty
bayes_return_levels = Matrix{Float64}(undef, length(μ_posterior), length(return_periods))
for i in 1:length(μ_posterior)
    for j in 1:length(return_periods)
        bayes_return_levels[i, j] = gev_return_level(
            μ_posterior[i], σ_posterior[i], ξ_posterior[i], return_periods[j])
    end
end
```

```{julia}
#| label: return-level-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Return level estimates with uncertainty bands from bootstrap and Bayesian approaches. Uncertainty increases substantially for longer return periods."

fig = Figure(size=(800, 600))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="Return Level Uncertainty Comparison",
    xlabel="Return Period (years)",
    ylabel="Return Level (mm)",
    xscale=log10)

# True return levels
lines!(ax, return_periods, true_return_levels,
    color=:red, linewidth=3, label="True")

# MLE point estimates
lines!(ax, return_periods, mle_return_levels,
    color=:black, linewidth=2, linestyle=:dash, label="MLE")

# Bootstrap confidence intervals
boot_lower = [quantile(boot_return_levels[:, j], 0.025) for j in 1:length(return_periods)]
boot_upper = [quantile(boot_return_levels[:, j], 0.975) for j in 1:length(return_periods)]
boot_mean = [mean(boot_return_levels[:, j]) for j in 1:length(return_periods)]

band!(ax, return_periods, boot_lower, boot_upper,
    color=(:blue, 0.3), label="Bootstrap 95% CI")
lines!(ax, return_periods, boot_mean,
    color=:blue, linewidth=2, label="Bootstrap")

# Bayesian credible intervals
bayes_lower = [quantile(bayes_return_levels[:, j], 0.025) for j in 1:length(return_periods)]
bayes_upper = [quantile(bayes_return_levels[:, j], 0.975) for j in 1:length(return_periods)]
bayes_mean = [mean(bayes_return_levels[:, j]) for j in 1:length(return_periods)]

band!(ax, return_periods, bayes_lower, bayes_upper,
    color=(:green, 0.3), label="Bayesian 95% CI")
lines!(ax, return_periods, bayes_mean,
    color=:green, linewidth=2, label="Bayesian")

ax.xticks = (return_periods, string.(return_periods))
axislegend(ax, position=:lt)
fig
```

## Uncertainty comparison table

Let's create a comprehensive comparison of uncertainty estimates:

```{julia}
#| label: uncertainty-table

# Calculate confidence/credible intervals for key return periods
key_return_periods = [10, 50, 100]

comparison_data = DataFrame(
    ReturnPeriod = key_return_periods,
    True = [gev_return_level(true_params.μ, true_params.σ, true_params.ξ, rp) for rp in key_return_periods],
    MLE = [gev_return_level(μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle, rp) for rp in key_return_periods]
)

# Add bootstrap intervals
bootstrap_lower = Float64[]
bootstrap_upper = Float64[]
for rp in key_return_periods
    rp_idx = findfirst(x -> x == rp, return_periods)
    boot_ci = quantile(boot_return_levels[:, rp_idx], [0.025, 0.975])
    push!(bootstrap_lower, boot_ci[1])
    push!(bootstrap_upper, boot_ci[2])
end
comparison_data.Bootstrap_Lower = bootstrap_lower
comparison_data.Bootstrap_Upper = bootstrap_upper

# Add Bayesian intervals
bayesian_lower = Float64[]
bayesian_upper = Float64[]
for rp in key_return_periods
    rp_idx = findfirst(x -> x == rp, return_periods)
    bayes_ci = quantile(bayes_return_levels[:, rp_idx], [0.025, 0.975])
    push!(bayesian_lower, bayes_ci[1])
    push!(bayesian_upper, bayes_ci[2])
end
comparison_data.Bayesian_Lower = bayesian_lower
comparison_data.Bayesian_Upper = bayesian_upper

# Round for display
for col in names(comparison_data)
    if eltype(comparison_data[!, col]) <: AbstractFloat
        comparison_data[!, col] = round.(comparison_data[!, col], digits=1)
    end
end

println("Return level uncertainty comparison:")
display(comparison_data)
```

## Uncertainty growth with return period

An important characteristic of extreme value analysis is how uncertainty grows with return period:

```{julia}
#| label: uncertainty-growth
#| fig-cap: "Relative uncertainty (coefficient of variation) increases with return period, highlighting the challenges of extrapolation to rare events."

# Calculate coefficient of variation (relative uncertainty)
boot_cv = [std(boot_return_levels[:, j]) / mean(boot_return_levels[:, j])
          for j in 1:length(return_periods)]
bayes_cv = [std(bayes_return_levels[:, j]) / mean(bayes_return_levels[:, j])
           for j in 1:length(return_periods)]

fig = Figure(size=(800, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="Relative Uncertainty vs. Return Period",
    xlabel="Return Period (years)",
    ylabel="Coefficient of Variation",
    xscale=log10)

lines!(ax, return_periods, boot_cv,
    color=:blue, linewidth=3, label="Bootstrap")
scatter!(ax, return_periods, boot_cv,
    color=:blue, markersize=8)

lines!(ax, return_periods, bayes_cv,
    color=:green, linewidth=3, label="Bayesian")
scatter!(ax, return_periods, bayes_cv,
    color=:green, markersize=8)

ax.xticks = (return_periods, string.(return_periods))
axislegend(ax, position=:lt)
fig
```

## Shape parameter sensitivity

The shape parameter ξ has the greatest influence on return level extrapolation. Let's visualize this sensitivity:

```{julia}
#| label: shape-sensitivity
#| fig-cap: "Sensitivity of 100-year return level to shape parameter ξ. Small changes in ξ lead to large changes in extrapolated return levels."

# Range of shape parameter values
ξ_range = -0.3:0.01:0.4
rp_100 = 100

# Calculate return levels for different ξ values (holding μ and σ fixed)
rl_sensitivity = [gev_return_level(μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ, rp_100) for ξ in ξ_range]

fig = Figure(size=(800, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="100-Year Return Level Sensitivity to Shape Parameter",
    xlabel="Shape Parameter ξ",
    ylabel="100-Year Return Level (mm)")

lines!(ax, ξ_range, rl_sensitivity,
    color=:navy, linewidth=3)

# Mark the MLE estimate
scatter!(ax, [ξ_mle], [gev_return_level(μ_mle, σ_mle, ξ_mle, rp_100)],
    color=:red, markersize=12, label="MLE estimate")

# Mark true value
scatter!(ax, [true_params.ξ], [gev_return_level(true_params.μ, true_params.σ, true_params.ξ, rp_100)],
    color=:green, markersize=12, label="True value")

# Add uncertainty band from Bayesian analysis
ξ_bayes_ci = quantile(ξ_posterior, [0.025, 0.975])
vspan!(ax, ξ_bayes_ci[1], ξ_bayes_ci[2], color=(:blue, 0.2),
    label="Bayesian 95% CI for ξ")

axislegend(ax, position=:lt)
fig
```

## Model checking with uncertainty

Finally, let's use posterior predictive checks to validate our uncertainty quantification:

```{julia}
#| label: posterior-predictive-check
#| fig-cap: "Posterior predictive check showing that observed data falls within the range of model predictions, validating our uncertainty quantification."

# Generate posterior predictive samples
n_pred_samples = 200
pred_samples = Matrix{Float64}(undef, n_pred_samples, n_years)

Random.seed!(456)
for i in 1:n_pred_samples
    # Sample parameters from posterior
    idx = rand(1:length(μ_posterior))
    μ_sample = μ_posterior[idx]
    σ_sample = σ_posterior[idx]
    ξ_sample = ξ_posterior[idx]

    # Generate synthetic data
    pred_samples[i, :] = rand(GeneralizedExtremeValue(μ_sample, σ_sample, ξ_sample), n_years)
end

# Calculate prediction intervals
pred_lower = [quantile(pred_samples[:, j], 0.025) for j in 1:n_years]
pred_upper = [quantile(pred_samples[:, j], 0.975) for j in 1:n_years]
pred_median = [quantile(pred_samples[:, j], 0.5) for j in 1:n_years]

fig = Figure(size=(800, 500))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title="Posterior Predictive Check",
    xlabel="Year",
    ylabel="Annual Maximum (mm)")

# Prediction intervals
band!(ax, 1:n_years, pred_lower, pred_upper,
    color=(:blue, 0.3), label="95% Prediction Interval")
lines!(ax, 1:n_years, pred_median,
    color=:blue, linewidth=2, label="Predicted Median")

# Observed data
scatter!(ax, 1:n_years, annual_maxima,
    color=:red, alpha=0.7, markersize=8, label="Observed")

axislegend(ax, position=:rt)
fig
```

## Summary

This notebook demonstrates several key points about uncertainty quantification in extreme value analysis:

### 1. Multiple sources of uncertainty
- Parameter estimation uncertainty is substantial, especially for the shape parameter
- This uncertainty propagates strongly to return level estimates
- Uncertainty grows dramatically with return period

### 2. Methods comparison
- Bootstrap and Bayesian approaches generally give similar results
- Bayesian methods provide full posterior distributions and natural model checking
- Bootstrap is simpler to implement but requires careful handling of optimization failures

### 3. Practical implications
- Always report uncertainty intervals, not just point estimates
- The shape parameter ξ drives most of the uncertainty in extrapolation
- Return level estimates become increasingly uncertain for longer return periods
- Model checking through posterior predictive checks is essential

This analysis emphasizes why extreme value analysis requires careful uncertainty quantification and why design decisions should account for this uncertainty rather than relying on point estimates alone.
